article.title: Chaos and Excess
article.content:
Prologue

had been a while since a renowned money manager trash-talked Fama’s efficient market hypothesis.

recently, the financial times interviewed Jean-Philippe Bouchaud [wiki] and it was a fun, informational read, especially due to the very english style of writing & Bouchaud’s sufficiently succinct explanation of how the state of the markets relies upon net liquidity in it at a given point in time.

circling similar premise, back in 2013, Michael Pettis wrote a piece on the rationality of financial markets—in the backdrop of Nobel Prize in Economics that was jointly awarded to Fama, Shiller, and Hansen, “For their empirical analysis of asset prices”—dwelling on how the diverse mix of investors & strategies quantify the rationality within markets, and while Pettis doesn’t manage a multibillion dollar quant fund, his words make a lot of sense.

Dan Sundheim recently came on the Cheeky Pint hosted by John Collison, and they were joined by Daniel Gross. Sundheim talked a lot about himself, his idiosyncrasies (waking up unwarranted at 3:00 AM), an earnings morning, how D1 hires/invests, public v/s private markets, etc. [he also spoke to David Rubenstein back in May this year. that conversation had very similar themes, and i would champion that conversation because of David’s seniority but Gross and Collison are just so much better at asking questions, clearly].

my favourite parts were toward the end when (1) John asked for any advice Dan would like to give to a young person trying to get into investing, and after naming a couple of books, and the value investor club website, he said—“the more you read, the better you are”. (2) after Dan mentioned Buffet’s letters, John talked about the original general partnership letters Buffet used to write from late 50s to late 70s, and how the young, ambitious, and unpolished Warren Buffet was on display there. here are those letters.

Abundance

the case for abundance is quite simple: we produce a lot, we keep producing forever, and since there’s enough for everybody, the only incentive to work will be for cumulative progress of humanity. survival and social mobility will cease to exist as intrinsic motivators for labour and output. governance will be solved. economics and the fields of social sciences will most likely cease to exist. the only worry will be of the emotional kind. concisely, we will all live in heaven.

the advent of LLMs, popularly back in 2022, propelled the peripheral optimism around establishing such a state in the world—prosperity on the back on next token prediction—further (after crypto carried it for a good couple of years). and the optimism was as strong as anything right up until mid 2025, when suddenly, the ai-funding spree, spearheaded by venture capitalists and private credit, found its way to an epiphany: these companies (or as everyone seems to call them now: frontier labs) need to make money and they’re not.

the problem with excess or abundance, observed historically, is that its existence often gets accompanied by chaos.

John Calhoun’s Universe 25

[detailed write-up] In a controlled experiment, by John Calhoun, simulating abundance, rats were given unlimited food, water, and shelter within a confined space. Initially, the population grew rapidly and social life appeared orderly. But as density increased, behaviour deteriorated. Dominant males became violent, weaker ones withdrew, mothers neglected their young, and infant mortality soared. Eventually, reproduction stopped entirely. A final generation known as “the beautiful ones” emerged—healthy in appearance but socially vacant, spending their lives grooming and eating while avoiding all interaction.

The colony’s collapse, despite perfect physical conditions, revealed that social and psychological stability depend on structure, roles, and purpose, not merely on material sufficiency. When these break down under purposeless abundance, complex social behaviour disintegrates, leading to apathy, alienation, and ultimately extinction.

1990s and the 1920s are back

(also, in Fashion)

apart from the comparisons with the dot com bubble of the mid nineties [i think Paul Tudor Jones was the first to talk of this to a major media outlet], times today are also being compared eerily with the great depression of 1929. a lot of credit actually goes to Aaron Ross Sorkin for coming up with his new book—1929. he did an impressive roadshow across news channels, podcasts, and conferences talking about the similarities between the two timelines, the grandeur of the 1920s, and of course how he isn’t sure, but the recession’s on the way. Ruchir Sharma spoke about Gold’s rally this year & how the inverse link between securities & gold has broken down (i don’t completely agree with the observation, yet).

one of the most interesting pieces to come out in the last one month is this Owen Lamont blogpost/article about “dumb money” (retail participation in the financial markets) and how your waiter’s investment account is a great economic indicator [here].

the Fashion segue

Major fashion houses—Chanel (Matthieu Blazy), Gucci, Dior (Jonathan Anderson), Versace (Dario Vitale), Bottega Veneta (Louise Trotter), more—have all had a change in the creative leadership in the past 10-12 months. with the most consequential of them all being of Chanel’s and Dior’s. well, especially, Dior’s.

when did something like this happen last time? in 1990s.

[Tom Ford at Gucci. Stella McCartney for Chloe. Michael Kors at Celine. Marc Jacobs for Louis Vuitton. John Galliano for Givenchy & Dior. Alexander McQueen at Givenchy.]

[a good article to read to know about this from someone who actually knows about fashion].

Miscellaneous*

  1. found this website to download high-quality digital scans of famous artworks.

  2. Vertasium’s video on Markov Chains. one of the most profound ideas in mathematics, with an equally (if not more) interesting origin as Calculus’.

  3. An Excerpt from Max Chafkin’s “The Contrarian”—[xeet here]:

    there’s also this article that came out right before the book, an absolute banger. [must read]

  1. Kris Abdelmessih’s blog. a lot of good writing. i got there via this one particularly: Lessons From Susquehanna.

  2. around late October, this image kept popping up on my x timeline. this image is of the connaught place, in 1970s Delhi. apart from the stark colours, absence of traffic, no street shops, and the lack of women in the photograph, one more thing might catch people’s eyes—it’s the fashion. everything from a business jacket and a peacoat to an indigo sweater and a leather jacket.

    it’s an incredible photograph.

  1. Also, I came across this article written by Siddharth Chowdhury in the economic times [25th October] and it was really fun to read.

  1. some youtube videos I watched:

    • Gustaf Ericson—COO, Citadel Securities, India [here]

    • A quant’s perspective on the 2008 financial crises [here]

    • Stanley Druckenmiller interview, 2009 [here]

    • The Citadel Finance Speaker series with Bill Ackman [here]

  2. I asked one of my seniors, about his favourite book on trading/investing. the answer was this series of books featuring interviews with traders about their experiences in the commodities, stock, and currency markets.

    • Market Wizards: Interviews with Top Traders, 1989 [pdf]

    • The New Market Wizards: Conversations with America’s Top Traders, 1992 [pdf]

    • Stock Market Wizards: Interviews with America’s Top Stock Traders, 2001 [pdf]

      I mean, there are two more books in this series [Hedge Fund Market Wizards: How Winning Traders Win, 2012 and Unknown Market Wizards: The Best Traders You’ve Never Heard Of, 2020] but he didn’t mention them specifically. I might read these soon.

    these are written by Jack Schwager—who himself, is an absolute legend.
